title: Suspected Elder Abuse + Neglect in 82-Year-Old Woman Admitted from Home with Stage 4 Sacral Pressure Injury, Severe Dehydration + Malnutrition, Patterned Bruising of Various Ages, and Untreated Urosepsis: Mandated Reporting (CA WIC §15630), Capacity-Sensitive Safety Planning, Trauma-Informed Documentation, and Multidisciplinary Coordination with Adult Protective Services
subtitle: RECOGNITION of PHYSICAL ABUSE cues (patterned bruising distribution + injuries inconsistent with stated history + various ages of healing) + NEGLECT cues (Stage 4 sacral pressure injury + dehydration BUN/Cr 78/2.4 + severe malnutrition albumin 1.9 + poor hygiene + untreated urosepsis); DIFFERENTIATE accidental injury + medical mimics (skin fragility, anticoagulant bruising, osteoporotic fracture) from abuse via DISTRIBUTION + PATTERN + HISTORY-INJURY MISMATCH + SOCIAL CONTEXT; SEPARATE INTERVIEW of patient from caregiver (private + safe); TRAUMA-INFORMED communication; CAPACITY ASSESSMENT (decision-specific — cognitive impairment does NOT eliminate patient autonomy; balance autonomy vs protection when capable patient refuses intervention); MANDATED REPORTING to APS within 24 hr by phone + 2 working days written (CA SOC 341); RN is MANDATED REPORTER in all 50 states — REASONABLE SUSPICION is the threshold (NOT proof; document objectively, not conclusively); SAFETY PLANNING (hospital as safe haven; do NOT discharge to suspected perpetrator; social work + APS + ombudsman + law enforcement if active danger; safe placement); OBJECTIVE DOCUMENTATION with descriptive language + body diagrams + photos with consent + verbatim quotes; INTERDISCIPLINARY coordination (social work + APS + geriatric medicine + wound + ethics + chaplain + law enforcement when indicated); CAREGIVER STRESS recognition (NOT excuse but context — referral to caregiver respite/support); SECONDARY VICTIMIZATION avoidance (avoid blame + interrogation + revictimization); LONG-TERM PLACEMENT planning post-acute (SNF + alternative caregiver + adult day program + IHSS + home health); HEALTH EQUITY considerations + cultural humility + interpreter services

patient.name: E. Calderón

patient.admitDate: EMS brought from home 1430 today after adult son called 911 saying mother 'fell yesterday and isn't waking up well'; on EMS arrival found patient lying on living room floor in soiled clothing + adult brief saturated + visible large open wound on sacrum + mottled extremities + Glasgow Coma Scale 12 (E3 V4 M5 — confused); EMS administered O2 + IV access + NS bolus 500 mL en route; ED 1455 → triage RN identified multiple concerning findings beyond fall + activated social work + APS reporting protocol; transferred to medicine inpatient unit Bed 3 at 2200 after ED stabilization (urosepsis bundle initiated, fluid resuscitation, wound consult activated, dietary consult, geriatric medicine consulted, social work + APS notified)

He doesn't mean to. But sometimes he grabs me. He's tired from caring for me.' — verbatim quote documented; PATIENT also stated: 'I don't want him in trouble. He's all I have.' — verbatim quote documented; DAUGHTER (lives in next state, age 56) arrived after ED notified — distraught + confused + states she has not been allowed to visit by son for 6 months ('he said Mom didn't want visitors and was too tired'); LAST PCP visit per records 14 months ago; LAST documented weight 18 months ago (52 kg → now 38 kg = 27% loss); MEDICATIONS per home: levothyroxine 75 mcg, amlodipine 5 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, donepezil 5 mg (for mild cognitive impairment); SON had been managing medications per family report — pill count on home meds brought in by EMS shows multiple doses missed

labs.v: 82 y/o female E. Calderón brought to ED 1430 today by EMS after adult son called 911 claiming mother 'fell yesterday and isn't waking up well.' STATED HISTORY (son): patient fell yesterday afternoon while trying to walk to bathroom; he helped her to floor mat where she has been since; he thought she would 'sleep it off'; he called 911 today when she did not respond well. INCONSISTENCIES with stated history: (1) STAGE 4 sacral pressure injury with visible bone + necrotic eschar = WEEKS TO MONTHS evolution, NOT acute fall, (2) severe contractures bilateral hands + R foot = months of immobility, (3) 27% weight loss over 18 months = chronic malnutrition, (4) bruising of various ages (yellow-green 10–14 days + purple-blue 3–5 days) = not single fall event, (5) bruising distribution (bilateral upper-arm grip patterns + posterior thigh handprint + behind ears) atypical for accidental/fall mechanism, (6) untreated urosepsis = significant indwelling time on floor in soiled brief without adequate care or medical attention, (7) prior outpatient records show last PCP visit 14 months ago + son had been blocking daughter's visits × 6 months. PATIENT (privately to ED RN with curtain pulled, son out of room — interpreter activated despite English functional per institutional protocol for sensitive interview): 'My son gets angry. He doesn't mean to. But sometimes he grabs me. He's tired from caring for me.' — verbatim documented. PATIENT also: 'I don't want him in trouble. He's all I have.' Patient denies sexual abuse on direct ask. Patient acknowledges does not always get meals or fluids when she asks. Patient acknowledges has not been to PCP because son 'doesn't have time to take me.' SON history: 58 y/o, unemployed × 2 years (former mechanic), lives with mother, sole caregiver since father (patient's husband) died 2 years ago; daughter reports son has history of alcohol use 'on and off' + financial dependence on mother (lives in her home + uses her Social Security/pension for shared expenses); no documented mental health diagnosis but daughter expresses concern about possible depression + isolation in son; family physician noted in chart 14 months ago 'caregiver appears overwhelmed; recommended caregiver support referral — declined by son.'
labs.r: HISTORY-INJURY MISMATCH is the central recognition principle for elder abuse + neglect. Stated history (single fall yesterday) cannot account for findings (chronic Stage 4 pressure injury, contractures, multiple-aged bruises, severe weight loss, untreated urosepsis, social isolation). DOCUMENT objective findings + verbatim patient quotes; AVOID conclusory language ('appears abused'); use descriptive neutral language. RN's role in OBSERVING + DOCUMENTING is independent of investigative determination — that is APS's role. RN is MANDATED REPORTER (CA WIC §15630 + all 50 states) — REASONABLE SUSPICION is the threshold, NOT proof.

labs.n: MANDATED REPORTING — CA WIC §15630 + APS coordination
labs.v: CA WELFARE & INSTITUTIONS CODE §15630 (Elder Abuse & Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act): designates MANDATED REPORTERS including healthcare practitioners (RNs, MDs, LVNs, LPTs, social workers, certified nursing assistants, EMTs, paramedics, dentists, optometrists, podiatrists, pharmacists, etc.). DEFINITIONS — ELDER = ≥65 yr; DEPENDENT ADULT = 18–64 with physical/mental limitations restricting ability to carry out normal activities or protect own rights. CATEGORIES of abuse: (1) PHYSICAL (assault, battery, unreasonable physical constraint, sexual abuse); (2) NEGLECT (failure to provide care, including failure to assist personal hygiene, provide food/clothing/shelter, provide medical care, protect from health/safety hazards, prevent malnutrition or dehydration) — INCLUDES self-neglect; (3) ABANDONMENT; (4) ISOLATION; (5) FINANCIAL ABUSE (taking/concealing/appropriating/retaining real or personal property); (6) ABDUCTION; (7) GOAL-OF-CARE deprivation. THRESHOLD for reporting = REASONABLE SUSPICION (NOT proof); 'reasonable to suspect' = objectively reasonable for a person in same position to entertain such suspicion. TIMELINE: PHONE REPORT within 24 hr to APS or local law enforcement; WRITTEN REPORT (SOC 341 form) within 2 WORKING DAYS to APS + ombudsman if facility-based + law enforcement if physical abuse; for SUSPECTED PHYSICAL ABUSE with serious injury — also call local law enforcement immediately. CONFIDENTIALITY/PRIVILEGE: mandated reporter is GRANTED IMMUNITY from civil/criminal liability for reports made in good faith; identity of reporter is CONFIDENTIAL (not disclosed without consent); FAILURE TO REPORT is a MISDEMEANOR (up to 6 mo jail + $1,000 fine; up to 1 yr + $5,000 if death/great bodily injury results from non-report). REPORTS GO TO: APS for community-dwelling adults; LONG-TERM CARE OMBUDSMAN for residential facility residents; LAW ENFORCEMENT for physical abuse + financial abuse + life-threatening situations + cases requiring immediate intervention. THIS CASE: ED RN called APS phone within 4 hr of recognition (1500 → APS 1830 with intake number documented); WRITTEN SOC 341 submitted within 24 hr; LAW ENFORCEMENT also notified (suspected physical abuse with serious injury + active safety concern); patient consent NOT REQUIRED for mandated report (overrides usual confidentiality + HIPAA permits disclosure per 45 CFR 164.512(c) for victims of abuse/neglect to government authority). APS INVESTIGATOR assigned + visited bedside 1100 next day; multi-disciplinary case conference scheduled.
labs.r: MANDATED REPORTING for suspected elder abuse is one of the most reliably tested NCLEX Ethical & Legal concepts. KEY POINTS: (1) RN is mandated reporter in ALL 50 states + applies to suspected/known abuse + REASONABLE SUSPICION is threshold (NOT proof), (2) PATIENT CONSENT NOT REQUIRED for mandated report (HIPAA exception 45 CFR 164.512(c)), (3) PHONE within 24 hr + WRITTEN within 2 working days (CA SOC 341), (4) IMMUNITY for good-faith reports; PENALTIES for failure to report, (5) DIFFERENT pathways for community-dwelling (APS), facility (ombudsman + APS), physical abuse (also law enforcement), (6) RN role is RECOGNIZE + DOCUMENT + REPORT — NOT investigate or determine if abuse occurred (APS investigates).

labs.n: RECOGNIZING types of elder abuse + neglect (signs + risk factors)
labs.v: TYPES of elder abuse + characteristic signs: (1) PHYSICAL ABUSE — bruising in unusual locations (inner thigh, behind ears, upper arms with grip marks, head, neck), bruising in PATTERNS (handprint, fingerprint, belt, cord, cigarette burns), bruising of DIFFERENT AGES suggesting repeated events, fractures inconsistent with history, defensive injuries, injuries on bilateral sides or symmetric, head injuries, missing teeth, signs of restraint use, rope/binding marks; (2) NEGLECT — pressure injuries (especially Stage 3–4 in immobile patient), dehydration, malnutrition (cachexia, weight loss), poor hygiene (long nails, matted hair, unwashed clothing/body, dental decay), incontinence-associated dermatitis severe + chronic, untreated medical conditions, missed medical appointments, medication non-administration (pill count mismatch), inappropriate clothing for weather, soiled bedding/environment; (3) SEXUAL ABUSE — bruising/bleeding in genital region, sexually transmitted infections in non-sexually-active patient, torn/stained/bloody undergarments, unexplained genital injuries; (4) EMOTIONAL/PSYCHOLOGICAL — depression, anxiety, withdrawal, fear/agitation around specific caregiver, rocking/mumbling, statements about being yelled at or threatened; (5) FINANCIAL — unexplained withdrawals/transfers, missing belongings, sudden changes in will/POA, unpaid bills despite resources, caregiver's lifestyle exceeds means, lack of necessities despite resources; (6) ABANDONMENT — left at hospital + family unreachable, left in public place; (7) SELF-NEGLECT — same signs as neglect by another but no caregiver involvement. RISK FACTORS for ELDER (victim): cognitive impairment/dementia, social isolation, dependence on others for ADLs/IADLs, female gender (more reported but men also victims), history of family violence, comorbid psychiatric illness, frailty, depression. RISK FACTORS for CAREGIVER (perpetrator): caregiver stress/burnout, mental health disorder (depression, substance use), financial dependence on elder, history of violence/abuse (cycle), social isolation, inadequate training/support, role reversal trauma, history of being abused by elder in past. THIS CASE checks MULTIPLE BOXES: physical abuse (patterned grip-mark bruising + bruises various ages + behind-ear bruising atypical), neglect (Stage 4 pressure injury + dehydration + malnutrition + untreated urosepsis + poor hygiene + medication non-administration + isolation from daughter), risk factors (cognitive impairment + social isolation + ADL dependence + female + son with substance use history + caregiver financial dependence + caregiver isolation).
labs.r: RECOGNITION is the central nursing skill in elder abuse cases. CUES include physical findings (location + pattern + age of injuries), neglect findings (chronic care gaps), behavioral findings (fear of caregiver, withdrawal), and history-injury mismatch. SOCIAL CONTEXT matters (caregiver risk factors + family dynamics + financial). NO SINGLE FINDING is diagnostic — pattern of findings + history-injury mismatch + social context together raise reasonable suspicion threshold for mandated reporting.

labs.n: MEDICAL MIMICS + DIFFERENTIAL — distinguishing abuse from accident or disease
labs.v: MEDICAL CONDITIONS that can MIMIC abuse + must be DIFFERENTIATED: (1) SKIN FRAGILITY in elderly — thin skin + decreased subcutaneous tissue + minor trauma causes bruising; pattern + location matter (purpura on dorsum of forearms/hands very common + bilateral + NOT abuse); (2) ANTICOAGULANT or antiplatelet medications — easy bruising; distinguishes from abuse by anticoag use + lab values (INR, PT, PTT) + distribution; (3) BLEEDING DISORDERS — von Willebrand, ITP, leukemia, liver disease — labs distinguish; (4) STEROID USE — thin skin + bruising; (5) OSTEOPOROTIC FRACTURES — common with minor trauma in elderly; vertebral compression, hip, distal radius are typical (suspicious patterns: bilateral upper-arm fractures, head/face fractures, multiple-age fractures, unusual location); (6) FALLS — common in elderly with cognitive impairment, polypharmacy, sarcopenia; pattern of injury matters (fall = same-side injuries usually + hands/knees/head usually + history consistent); (7) ARTHRITIS/CONTRACTURES — can occur with inactivity or neurologic disease (e.g., stroke); rapid contracture development in functional patient suggests neglect; (8) MALNUTRITION + WEIGHT LOSS — also from malignancy, depression, chronic illness, dementia (refusing food); medical workup distinguishes; (9) DERMATOLOGIC conditions — mongolian spots (rare in elderly), purpura senilis, dermatitis artefacta. KEY DISTINGUISHERS: (1) HISTORY-INJURY MISMATCH (mechanism cannot explain finding), (2) PATTERN of injury (grip marks, handprints, defensive injuries, geometric burns, ligature marks), (3) DISTRIBUTION (typical accidental = anterior, bilateral hands/knees/head; suspicious = inner thigh, behind ears, upper arms, neck, genitals, posterior body), (4) MULTIPLE AGES of injuries suggesting repeated events, (5) DELAYED PRESENTATION of significant injury, (6) INCONSISTENT history (changes between sources or over time), (7) SOCIAL CONTEXT (caregiver behavior, isolation, dependence), (8) PATIENT BEHAVIOR (fear, withdrawal, statements). THIS CASE distinguishers: (a) Stage 4 pressure injury cannot result from yesterday's fall — chronic process; (b) bruising distribution (behind ears, upper arms with grip marks, posterior thigh) atypical for accidental fall; (c) bruises of different ages = not single event; (d) contractures bilateral = months of immobility; (e) 27% weight loss = chronic; (f) caregiver claim of acute fall does NOT explain chronic findings; (g) patient verbatim statement of caregiver grabbing.
labs.r: DIFFERENTIATING abuse from medical/accidental causes requires careful assessment + DOCUMENTATION of findings + history + social context. NO SINGLE finding is diagnostic; PATTERN matters. RN role is to DESCRIBE OBJECTIVELY + document — not adjudicate; APS investigates. REASONABLE SUSPICION threshold = if reasonable person in same position would suspect — REPORT. False-negative (missed abuse) far more harmful than false-positive (suspected but unconfirmed). Document descriptively; avoid conclusory language.

labs.v: DECISION-SPECIFIC CAPACITY assessment is essential. CAPACITY ≠ COMPETENCY (competency is legal; capacity is clinical + decision-specific). CAPACITY criteria: (1) UNDERSTAND relevant information (the decision + alternatives + risks + benefits), (2) APPRECIATE the situation + consequences (how info applies to them), (3) REASON through information (logical processing), (4) EXPRESS a stable choice. CAPACITY is DECISION-SPECIFIC — patient may have capacity for some decisions + not others; e.g., capable of consenting to treatment but not managing finances. COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT does NOT automatically eliminate capacity — formal assessment required. SLUMS, MoCA, MMSE screen for cognitive impairment but do NOT directly determine capacity; capacity is a clinical interview + decision-specific. RN role: gather data, observe + document patient understanding + choices, raise capacity questions to team, advocate for formal capacity assessment when concerns arise. IF CAPACITY PRESENT + patient REFUSES intervention/placement away from suspected abuser: (a) MANDATED REPORTING still mandatory (RN role is recognition + report; APS investigates regardless of patient's stated wishes); (b) RESPECT autonomy — competent adult has right to refuse intervention; (c) PROVIDE EDUCATION about resources + safety planning + how to access help if circumstances change; (d) ENGAGE SOCIAL WORK + APS to support patient autonomy while monitoring; (e) DOCUMENT capacity assessment + patient's informed choice + resources offered. IF CAPACITY ABSENT: (a) seek surrogate decision-maker (healthcare proxy if designated; if not, statutory hierarchy = spouse > adult child > parent > sibling > close friend); (b) public guardian/conservator if no surrogate or surrogate conflict; (c) APS engaged for vulnerable adult protection; (d) court intervention possible (temporary conservatorship for emergency). THIS CASE: Mrs. Calderón is oriented person + place + situation; baseline mild cognitive impairment per family + donepezil for MCI/early dementia; formal SLUMS/MoCA pending when medically stable. Her verbatim statement 'I don't want him in trouble. He's all I have' reflects her values + relationships; this is COMMON in elder abuse (loyalty, dependence, fear of nursing-home placement, isolation). MANDATED REPORTING is REQUIRED regardless of her stated wishes. BUT placement + ongoing intervention require capacity-sensitive engagement: if she has capacity + chooses to return to son after APS investigation + safety plan, that is HER decision; if capacity absent, alternative placement decisions made via surrogate or conservatorship. KEY TENSION: mandated reporting is non-negotiable + protects patient + invokes system; ongoing intervention navigates AUTONOMY + PROTECTION.
labs.r: AUTONOMY-PROTECTION balance is a high-yield NCLEX ethics concept. KEY POINTS: (1) capacity is decision-specific + clinical, (2) cognitive impairment ≠ no capacity, (3) MANDATED REPORTING is REQUIRED regardless of patient wishes (overrides autonomy for the reporting itself), (4) ongoing intervention/placement respects autonomy if capacity present, (5) loyalty/fear/dependence are common reasons capable victims refuse intervention — DO NOT PRESSURE or shame, (6) safety planning + resources + monitoring continue even with refusal, (7) surrogate decision-making + conservatorship pathways for incapable patients. RN advocates for both autonomy + protection — these are not opposites but require nuanced navigation.

labs.v: TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE principles: (1) SAFETY — physical + emotional safety; private + uninterrupted setting; never with suspected perpetrator present; (2) TRUSTWORTHINESS + TRANSPARENCY — explain process, your role, what happens with information; (3) PEER SUPPORT — connect with advocates, survivors; (4) COLLABORATION + MUTUALITY — sharing power; (5) EMPOWERMENT + VOICE + CHOICE — patient decides what to share + when + how; (6) CULTURAL/HISTORICAL/GENDER awareness — humility + responsive care. PRACTICAL INTERVIEW principles: (a) SEPARATE patient from caregiver — private room, screen pulled, caregiver politely asked to step out 'for a routine private medical interview'; (b) USE PROFESSIONAL INTERPRETER if language barrier, NOT family member as interpreter (especially not the suspected perpetrator); (c) BUILD RAPPORT first with neutral conversation; (d) USE OPEN-ENDED + non-leading questions ('Can you tell me about your daily life?' 'Who helps you at home?' 'How do you feel when X happens?'); (e) ASK DIRECTLY but supportively about safety ('Sometimes when people are dependent on others, things become hard. Has anyone hurt you or made you feel unsafe?' 'Sometimes caregivers get frustrated. Has that happened with you?'); (f) USE NEUTRAL ACCEPTING LANGUAGE — never judgment of patient OR caregiver; (g) DOCUMENT VERBATIM in quotes when significant; (h) RESPECT PACE — patient may need multiple conversations; (i) DO NOT INTERROGATE — gather data + observations + report; APS investigates; (j) RESPECT cultural values + family dynamics; (k) AVOID secondary victimization — patient is NOT to blame + did not cause this; (l) ACKNOWLEDGE patient feelings; (m) PROVIDE INFORMATION about resources without coercion; (n) DOCUMENT objective findings + verbatim quotes + your observations + what you did. AVOID: blaming patient ('Why did you let this happen?'), interrogating caregiver, leading questions ('Did he hit you?' before patient discloses), promising not to report (you ARE mandated), making conclusory statements in the chart ('appears abused'). DAUGHTER as collateral: gather information about home environment + son's behavior + recent changes + her own observations; supportive role; she may also need emotional support + resources (sibling guilt + grief).
labs.r: TRAUMA-INFORMED CARE is the framework for engaging victims of abuse. KEY POINTS: (1) SAFETY first (separate from suspected perpetrator), (2) PROFESSIONAL INTERPRETER not family member, (3) OPEN-ENDED non-leading questions, (4) DIRECT but supportive ask about safety, (5) verbatim documentation, (6) DO NOT INTERROGATE — observe + report; APS investigates, (7) AVOID secondary victimization. RN role = OBSERVE + DOCUMENT + REPORT + SUPPORT — NOT investigate.

labs.v: HOSPITAL as SAFE HAVEN: admission provides physical safety + medical stabilization + investigation window + multidisciplinary engagement. SAFETY PLAN includes: (1) RESTRICTED VISITATION of suspected perpetrator pending APS investigation (handled by social work explanation to patient + family; security notified; bedside sign; nursing aware); (2) PRIVATE ROOM if available; (3) STAFF AWARENESS via shift report; (4) DOCUMENTATION + photos with consent + body diagrams; (5) MULTIDISCIPLINARY case conference (social work + medicine + nursing + geriatrics + wound + nutrition + ethics + APS + chaplain + ombudsman if applicable + law enforcement if active danger). POST-ACUTE PLACEMENT options: (a) SKILLED NURSING FACILITY (SNF) — for wound healing + rehabilitation + monitoring; capacity-sensitive decision; (b) ALTERNATIVE CAREGIVER — daughter (lives in next state) considering relocating temporarily or moving mother in with her; daughter must understand demands + receive caregiver support; (c) ADULT DAY HEALTH CARE — if returning home with restructured caregiving; (d) IN-HOME SUPPORTIVE SERVICES (IHSS) — California Medi-Cal program providing caregiver hours for vulnerable adults — APPLICATION can be made; can hire a non-family caregiver paid via IHSS; (e) HOME HEALTH NURSING + WOUND CARE + PT/OT — Medicare home health coverage; (f) ASSISTED LIVING — if can afford; (g) CONSERVATORSHIP if capacity absent + no appropriate surrogate; (h) CARE COORDINATION via geriatric care manager. CAREGIVER STRESS recognition + intervention for son: NOT excuse for abuse but CONTEXT for prevention of recurrence; (a) caregiver support group; (b) respite care funding; (c) caregiver education on age-related care needs; (d) mental health + substance use treatment referral if applicable; (e) family meeting with daughter + son if APS investigation appropriate; (f) financial counseling if dependency contributing. PROTECTION DURING ADMISSION: (a) DO NOT discharge to suspected perpetrator without safety plan + APS clearance; (b) court protective order possible if active danger + filed by APS or family or victim through court; (c) social work + APS coordinate placement decision with patient (if capacity) or surrogate. THIS CASE: pending APS investigation; patient currently medically stabilizing; visitation restricted; daughter at bedside; placement decision will be made jointly with patient (capacity-sensitive) + APS + social work + medical team + daughter; likely SNF for wound + nutrition + functional rehab × 4–8 weeks, then placement decision (return home with restructured caregiving OR daughter's home OR alternative).
labs.r: SAFETY PLANNING + post-acute placement require multidisciplinary coordination + capacity-sensitive engagement with patient. HOSPITAL is a SAFE HAVEN for investigation + planning. DO NOT discharge to suspected perpetrator without clearance. Multiple post-acute options. Caregiver stress is CONTEXT (not excuse) — addressing it prevents recurrence. Court protective orders possible. RN advocacy + documentation + coordination central.

labs.n: WOUND care + medical management — Stage 4 sacral pressure injury
labs.v: STAGE 4 SACRAL PRESSURE INJURY 8 × 10 cm with visible bone + necrotic eschar at edges + foul odor + purulent drainage. ASSESSMENT: length × width × depth + undermining + tunneling + tissue type (granulation/slough/eschar/bone) + exudate amount + color + odor + periwound skin; PHOTOGRAPHY with patient consent + body diagram; pain assessment (often masked by neuropathy + cognitive impairment). MANAGEMENT: (1) WOUND CARE consult activated; (2) DEBRIDEMENT — sharp (surgical or by certified wound nurse) + autolytic (hydrogel) + enzymatic (collagenase) + biological (maggot) options; this Stage 4 with eschar requires sharp debridement evaluation; OR for OR debridement + possible flap reconstruction if extensive + viable patient; (3) DRESSING — varies by tissue type; this wound = clean + pack with saline gauze or alginate for exudate management + non-adherent secondary; consider negative-pressure wound therapy (NPWT/wound vac) post-debridement; (4) OFFLOADING — pressure redistribution mattress (alternating pressure + low-air-loss); q2h repositioning (avoiding sacrum); side-lying with 30° angle; heel offloading; (5) NUTRITION — high-protein + caloric supplementation (Ensure/Boost or registered dietitian advanced protocol); albumin 1.9 reflects severe protein malnutrition; tube feeding consideration if oral intake inadequate (capacity-sensitive); vitamin C + zinc supplementation for wound healing; (6) INFECTION — osteomyelitis likely with visible bone; bone biopsy + culture; IV antibiotics (this patient — cefepime + vancomycin for empiric broad coverage); pending cultures; ID consult; expected 6+ weeks IV antibiotic for osteomyelitis; (7) PAIN management with appropriate analgesia. UROSEPSIS bundle: blood + urine cultures (Foley urine sent on placement); broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (cefepime + vancomycin); fluid resuscitation; source control (Foley placement for drainage); lactate trending; vasopressor PRN. MALNUTRITION: nutrition consult + albumin + prealbumin + vitamins + minerals; refeeding syndrome risk in severe malnutrition (start slow + monitor phos/K/Mg); oral supplements + advance diet as tolerated; tube feeding decision capacity-sensitive. DEHYDRATION: ongoing fluid management based on BUN/Cr trending + I/O + clinical assessment. POLYPHARMACY review: medications were not being administered correctly at home; reconcile + restart as appropriate; deprescribe non-essential; manage in-hospital appropriately. PHYSICAL therapy + occupational therapy: contracture stretching, ROM, mobility progression as tolerated. DEPRESSION + COGNITIVE assessment when stable: SLUMS/MoCA when medically stable; geriatric depression scale; cognitive baseline establishment for capacity assessment + post-acute planning.
labs.r: MEDICAL MANAGEMENT of severe neglect-related complications (Stage 4 pressure injury + urosepsis + severe malnutrition + dehydration) is part of acute care alongside abuse investigation. WOUND CARE + NUTRITION + INFECTION management + REHABILITATION are central. ALL of this is documented as clinical findings — connected to but separate from the reporting/investigation process which is APS's role.

labs.v: DOCUMENTATION PRINCIPLES for suspected abuse: (1) OBJECTIVE + DESCRIPTIVE — describe what you see, not your interpretation; 'multiple bruises in different stages of healing on bilateral upper arms in pattern consistent with 4-finger grip marks' NOT 'patient appears to have been grabbed'; 'Stage 4 sacral pressure injury 8 × 10 cm with visible bone and necrotic eschar' NOT 'severely neglected wound'; (2) BODY DIAGRAMS with annotation of every finding (location + size + color + pattern + age); (3) PHOTOGRAPHY with patient consent; standardized protocol (rulers/scale in photo, identifying marker, dated/timed, consent documented); photos to be uploaded to medical record + accessible for APS investigation; (4) VERBATIM QUOTES from patient + family + caregiver in quotation marks ('My son gets angry. He doesn't mean to. But sometimes he grabs me.'); (5) SOURCE attribution ('patient stated...' 'son stated...' 'daughter stated...'); (6) HISTORY-INJURY MISMATCH documented; (7) TIMELINE of events documented; (8) ALL CONSULTS + REFERRALS + REPORTS documented with date/time/recipient ('Phone report to APS at 1830 today; intake number 2026-0584321; reported to APS investigator J. Martinez'); (9) MANDATED REPORTING completion documented (SOC 341 form filed 0930 next day; copy in chart); (10) AVOID conclusory language ('abused' 'neglected' 'appears battered'); (11) DESCRIBE behavior + interactions ('patient turned away from son when he entered the room and began rocking back and forth'); (12) MULTIPLE assessments over time — patient + family may disclose more or less at different points; document each. CONFIDENTIALITY: chart documentation is part of medical record; APS has access for investigation per CA WIC; HIPAA permits disclosure to government authority for victims of abuse per 45 CFR 164.512(c); mandated reporter identity remains confidential per CA WIC §15633.5. PHOTOGRAPHIC PROTOCOL: standardized institutional protocol; photos taken by RN with witness; placed in secure electronic medical record; available for APS investigation + legal proceedings. THIS CASE: ED RN documentation included full head-to-toe with body diagram + 12 photographs (with patient consent at time of capacity); verbatim patient quotes; verbatim caregiver quotes; APS phone report at 1830 ED Day 1 documented; SOC 341 written report submitted 0930 Day 2 documented; consult notes from social work + geriatric medicine + ethics + wound + nutrition + chaplain all in chart; law enforcement contact documented (officer interview Day 2); APS investigator visit Day 2 documented.
labs.r: OBJECTIVE DESCRIPTIVE DOCUMENTATION + verbatim quotes + photographs + body diagrams + timeline create the clinical record that supports BOTH patient safety + APS investigation + potential legal proceedings. AVOID conclusory language. DESCRIBE what you observe + document what was said. RN's documentation is independent of investigative determination — that is APS's role.

labs.v: MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM members for elder abuse case: (1) RN — bedside care, monitoring, advocacy, documentation, mandated reporting, communication, coordination; (2) HOSPITALIST + GERIATRICIAN — medical management, capacity assessment, comprehensive geriatric assessment; (3) SOCIAL WORK — psychosocial assessment, family liaison, resource coordination, APS coordination, placement planning, family meetings; (4) APS INVESTIGATOR — investigation, safety planning, post-acute coordination, resource linkage, monitoring, court involvement if needed; (5) WOUND CARE SPECIALIST — pressure injury management; (6) NUTRITION/DIETITIAN — malnutrition management + supplementation + refeeding syndrome prevention; (7) PT + OT — contracture/mobility/ADL rehabilitation; (8) INFECTIOUS DISEASE — sepsis + likely osteomyelitis management; (9) PSYCHIATRY/GERIATRIC PSYCHIATRY — depression/anxiety screening + treatment; capacity assessment input; (10) CHAPLAIN — spiritual support; (11) ETHICS CONSULT — capacity, autonomy-protection balance, end-of-life if applicable; (12) PALLIATIVE CARE — symptom management + goals of care; (13) LAW ENFORCEMENT — physical abuse investigation + emergency protective orders if active danger; (14) LONG-TERM CARE OMBUDSMAN — if patient resided in facility; (15) ELDER LAW ATTORNEY — civil action options; (16) CASE MANAGER — discharge planning, post-acute placement, payer coordination; (17) PHARMACY — medication reconciliation + deprescribing review; (18) INTERPRETER SERVICES — professional language access. FAMILY: (a) DAUGHTER (collaborative party) — supportive role, information source, potential alternative caregiver, also needs emotional support; (b) SON (suspected perpetrator) — handled per safety plan; APS investigation; access to bedside restricted pending investigation; access not denied permanently but managed during acute window; if APS finds substantiated abuse, ongoing access decisions made with patient consent + safety plan + possible protective orders; if found unsubstantiated, family reunification considered with caregiver support + monitoring; if criminal charges filed, courts may involve; (c) OTHER FAMILY — gathered + engaged. FAMILY MEETINGS: structured + facilitated by social work + geriatric medicine; include patient (with interpreter); explain medical findings + multidisciplinary team + APS investigation + safety plan + post-acute options; honor patient voice + autonomy. INFORMATION SHARING: HIPAA permits disclosure to APS + law enforcement per abuse exceptions; family information shared per patient permission + need-to-know basis; suspected perpetrator does NOT receive privileged information that could harm investigation.
labs.r: INTERDISCIPLINARY COORDINATION is essential for complex elder abuse cases. EVERY discipline contributes a piece. RN's bedside role is central in coordination + advocacy + documentation. FAMILY coordination is nuanced — supportive family members engaged collaboratively; suspected perpetrator handled per safety plan + investigation. APS leads investigation; clinical team supports + provides medical care + safety + documentation.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: no signs of sexual abuse on exam or by patient report; no obvious financial exploitation evident in records (will be investigated by APS); patient has supportive daughter who has been blocked from visiting × 6 months but is now actively engaged + appropriate; no current immediate life-threatening danger to patient in hospital setting (safety plan + restricted visitation); no signs of self-inflicted injury or attempted suicide; patient is medically stabilizing with appropriate resuscitation + antibiotics + wound care + nutrition; cognitive function recovering with treatment of urosepsis + dehydration (baseline mild cognitive impairment per family but improved from admission GCS 12 → now 15); patient has decision-specific capacity for most decisions per preliminary assessment + has stated preferences clearly; daughter is willing alternative caregiver / resource; insurance coverage active (Medicare + Medi-Cal supplement); home preserved currently for potential return if safe + appropriate; APS engaged + responsive; law enforcement engaged + responsive (filed report); team alignment + multidisciplinary engagement strong; chaplain support; interpreter services available; ethics consult available. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: hospital safety; supportive daughter; APS engagement; insurance coverage; supportive multidisciplinary team; chaplain + spiritual resources; cognitive function recovering; patient's clear voice + values.
labs.r: Despite severe findings, multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS + reassuring elements exist. Patient is in safe environment with multidisciplinary support. Daughter is engaged collaborator. APS responsive. Recovery + safety planning possible. These factors guide optimistic but realistic care.

nursesNote: 1455 — 82 y/o female E. Calderón (Spanish primary + English-functional; primary language Spanish per patient preference; professional Spanish interpreter activated for all medical decisions + sensitive conversations per institutional protocol — daughter not used as primary translator) brought to ED via EMS at 1430 after adult son called 911 saying mother 'fell yesterday and isn't waking up well'; EMS found patient on living room floor in soiled clothing + adult brief saturated × estimated 12+ hr with large open sacral wound visible + mottled extremities + GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5). EMS resuscitated en route (O2, IV access, NS bolus 500 mL); ED arrival 1455 vitals HR 118 sinus tachy BP 82/48 MAP 59 → 30 mL/kg NS resuscitation 1500–1700 → BP 100/58 MAP 72; SpO2 92% RA → 96% 2L NC; T 35.4°C HYPOthermia (sepsis + environmental exposure); weight 38 kg (down from 52 kg per outpatient PCP record 18 months ago = 27% loss = severe malnutrition); GCS recovering with resuscitation 12 → 13 → 14 → 15 by Day 2. ED HEAD-TO-TOE assessment by RN + ED physician + social work CALLED in early at triage given multiple concerning findings: STAGE 4 SACRAL PRESSURE INJURY 8 × 10 cm with visible bone + necrotic eschar at edges + foul odor + purulent drainage (CHRONIC weeks-to-months evolution NOT acute fall); multiple smaller pressure injuries (Stage 2 bilateral heels + Stage 2 R greater trochanter); severe cachexia + temporal wasting + sunken eyes; poor oral hygiene + broken teeth; long matted hair + long nails (no recent grooming); BRUISING bilateral upper arms in 4-finger GRIP MARK PATTERN in DIFFERENT AGES (yellow-green 10–14 days + purple-blue 3–5 days = MULTIPLE EVENTS, NOT single fall); bruising posterior thighs in oval/handprint pattern (uncommon site for accidental injury); bruising behind both ears (atypical for fall mechanism); healed scar R forehead linear ~3 cm (family/son did not report previously); parchment-thin skin with multiple skin tears (consistent with both fragility AND rough handling); CONTRACTURES bilateral hands + R foot (not noted in outpatient records 18 months ago = developed during prolonged immobility); severe incontinence-associated dermatitis perineum + buttocks; Foley placed on arrival — urine cloudy + foul + dark amber + 80 mL initial = severe oliguria; URINE labs: WBC >100/HPF + bacteria 4+ + nitrite + leukocyte esterase + (urosepsis); BLOOD cultures × 2 sent; sepsis bundle initiated (cefepime + vancomycin broad empiric, lactate, MAP target, fluid resuscitation); BMP: BUN 78 Cr 2.4 (acute kidney injury from sepsis + dehydration; baseline Cr 0.9 per outpatient records); Na 152 (severe hypernatremia from dehydration); K 3.1 + Mg 1.4 + Phos 1.8 (severe malnutrition + refeeding syndrome risk); albumin 1.9 + prealbumin 6 (severe protein malnutrition); Hgb 9.4 (anemia of chronic disease + malnutrition); lactate 4.8 (sepsis + hypoperfusion). HISTORY-INJURY MISMATCH RECOGNIZED by ED RN within first hour: STAGE 4 wound + contractures + 27% weight loss + bruises of varied ages + history of single yesterday's fall = inconsistent; chronic neglect + acute on chronic findings + suspicious bruising distribution. PRIVATE INTERVIEW with patient (son asked to step out for 'routine private medical interview'; Spanish interpreter present per institutional protocol despite English-functional — sensitive interview): patient verbalized 'My son gets angry. He doesn't mean to. But sometimes he grabs me. He's tired from caring for me.' — VERBATIM documented. Patient also: 'I don't want him in trouble. He's all I have.' — verbatim documented. Patient denied sexual abuse on direct supportive ask. Patient acknowledged does not always get meals or fluids when she asks. Patient acknowledged has not been to PCP because son 'doesn't have time to take me.' MANDATED REPORTING per CA WIC §15630 initiated: PHONE REPORT to APS at 1830 (within 4 hr of recognition) with intake number documented; PHONE REPORT to local law enforcement at 1900 (suspected physical abuse with serious injury); WRITTEN REPORT SOC 341 form completed + submitted 0930 next day; ED MD also reported (multiple mandated reporters; each independent); ETHICS consult activated; PALLIATIVE care consult activated (early integration); CHAPLAIN consult (Catholic — patient identifies); GERIATRIC MEDICINE consult; WOUND care consult; NUTRITION/DIETITIAN consult (refeeding syndrome risk + supplementation plan + capacity-sensitive feeding decisions); INFECTIOUS DISEASE consult (osteomyelitis likely with bone exposure — bone biopsy + culture planned + expected 6+ weeks IV antibiotics); SOCIAL WORK lead coordinator; APS investigator J. Martinez assigned; LAW ENFORCEMENT officer interview Day 2. SAFETY PLAN initiated: SON visitation RESTRICTED pending APS investigation — handled by social work explanation to patient + family; SECURITY notified + bedside sign; SHIFT REPORT awareness; PRIVATE ROOM Bed 3; DAUGHTER (lives in next state, age 56) arrived 1830 ED Day 1 after ED notified — distraught but supportive + appropriate; daughter reports son blocked her visits × 6 months claiming 'mother didn't want visitors and was too tired'; daughter is potential alternative caregiver / resource being assessed. PHOTOGRAPHIC PROTOCOL completed (institutional protocol; patient consent obtained at time of decision-specific capacity; 12 photographs of pressure injury + bruising + body habitus with rulers/scale + identifying marker + dated/timed + secured in EMR); BODY DIAGRAM completed with annotation of every finding (location + size + color + pattern + age). VERBATIM QUOTES documented as above; SOURCE attribution maintained throughout. CAPACITY assessment: patient oriented person + place + situation; baseline mild cognitive impairment per family + donepezil for MCI/early dementia; SLUMS/MoCA pending when medically stable; current decision-specific capacity assessment by ED + admitting MD = capable for participation in current decisions about treatment + disclosure; values + preferences clearly stated; loyalty to son acknowledged without invalidating safety concern; AUTONOMY-PROTECTION balance respected — mandated reporting non-negotiable + patient's voice respected in ongoing planning. MEDICAL MANAGEMENT: SEPSIS bundle (cefepime + vancomycin broad empiric; lactate trending; MAP ≥65; fluid resuscitation continued; source control via Foley + wound care; vasopressor PRN if MAP not maintained); WOUND care consult activated — Stage 4 sacral wound cleansed + packed with saline gauze + alginate for exudate + non-adherent secondary; ORTHO/wound care surgery evaluation for sharp debridement + possible flap reconstruction post-stabilization; pressure-redistribution mattress + alternating pressure; q2h repositioning AVOIDING sacrum + with 30° side-lying angle + heel offloading; smaller pressure injuries managed with appropriate dressings; NUTRITION — refeeding syndrome PRECAUTIONS in severe malnutrition with phos/K/Mg low at admission — start at 50% of calculated needs + advance by 10% per day if labs stable + replace electrolytes BEFORE feeding initiated; high-protein + caloric supplementation oral Ensure 3×/day; vitamin C 500 mg + zinc 220 mg daily for wound healing; multivitamin; thiamine 100 mg IV daily × 5 days BEFORE carbohydrate load (Wernicke prevention); tube feeding decision deferred capacity-sensitive + medical indication; DEHYDRATION resuscitation continued cautiously (severe hypernatremia 152 — correct slowly at no more than 10 mEq/L/24 hr to avoid cerebral edema); POLYPHARMACY review — restart levothyroxine 75 mcg + amlodipine 5 mg + atorvastatin 40 mg + donepezil 5 mg in hospital with verified administration; held during sepsis or as indicated; deprescribing review at discharge; AKI management — adjust antibiotic dosing per renal function; trend Cr + UOP; PT/OT bedside ROM + contracture management + mobility progression as tolerated; HBA1C + lipid panel + TSH + B12 + folate sent; CT head non-contrast on admission (r/o subdural from prior head injury + baseline) — small healing R frontal subdural ~2 weeks old + chronic atrophy; consistent with prior unreported trauma + age; no acute intervention needed but documented. FAMILY COMMUNICATION: daughter at bedside actively engaged; receives medical updates via family meeting + RN + social work + interpreter when needed; son receiving updates via social work only per safety plan; daughter expressed grief + guilt + commitment to mother's safety + considering temporary leave from work to support; daughter agrees with safety plan + investigation; family meeting scheduled Day 3 with palliative care + social work + geriatric medicine + APS + patient + daughter + son (depending on APS investigation status). DOCUMENTATION objective + descriptive + photographic + verbatim + sourced + timeline maintained throughout; AVOID conclusory language (no 'appears abused'); describe what observed. PLAN: medical stabilization + sepsis treatment + wound care + nutrition + rehabilitation + capacity assessment when stable + APS investigation + multidisciplinary case conference + safety planning + post-acute placement decision (likely SNF for 4–8 weeks of wound healing + nutrition + functional rehabilitation + then placement decision in coordination with patient + APS + social work + daughter — options include SNF long-term + daughter's home with caregiver support + restructured home environment with IHSS + alternative caregiver + adult day program); chaplain support + ethics consult + family meetings; caregiver stress addressed for son via APS coordination + mental health/substance use referral if applicable; ongoing monitoring + safety planning; team debriefing for emotional support of clinical team.
providerOrders: ADMIT to Medicine Telemetry Unit Bed 3 (private room for safety + privacy; visitor screening per safety plan)

DIAGNOSIS: severe sepsis/urosepsis from untreated UTI; Stage 4 sacral pressure injury with osteomyelitis (suspected); severe protein-calorie malnutrition with refeeding syndrome risk; severe dehydration with hypernatremia + AKI; acute on chronic functional decline; chronic mild cognitive impairment baseline; SUSPECTED elder abuse + neglect — APS investigation in progress

CODE STATUS: FULL CODE on admission; advance directives never completed; capacity assessment ongoing; healthcare decisions made by patient with appropriate decision-specific capacity assessment + interpreter for medical decisions
SAFETY PLAN:
Son visitation RESTRICTED pending APS investigation — social work communication with patient + family
Security notified + bedside sign + shift report awareness
Private room
Daughter (collaborative family member) permitted at bedside per patient consent
Photography protocol with patient consent (rulers/scale + identifying marker + dated/timed + EMR secure)
Body diagram with annotation of every finding
Verbatim quotes documented + source-attributed
MANDATED REPORTING (CA WIC §15630):
PHONE REPORT to Adult Protective Services within 24 hr of recognition — DONE 1830 ED Day 1; intake number documented
WRITTEN REPORT SOC 341 form within 2 working days — DONE 0930 Day 2; copy in chart
PHONE REPORT to local law enforcement for suspected physical abuse with serious injury — DONE 1900 ED Day 1
Mandated reporter immunity per CA WIC §15634; identity confidential per §15633.5
No patient consent required for mandated reporting (HIPAA exception 45 CFR 164.512(c))
INTERDISCIPLINARY consults activated:
Social Work (lead coordinator)
Geriatric Medicine
Wound Care Specialist
Infectious Disease (likely osteomyelitis + sepsis)
Nutrition/Dietitian (refeeding syndrome PRECAUTIONS + supplementation plan)
PT + OT (contractures + mobility + ADLs)
Psychiatry/Geriatric Psychiatry (depression + anxiety screening + capacity input)
Ethics Consult (autonomy-protection balance)
Palliative Care (symptom management + goals of care + supportive presence)
Chaplain (Catholic — patient preference)
Spanish Medical Interpreter (professional; daughter not used as primary translator)
Pharmacy (medication reconciliation + deprescribing)
Case Management (discharge planning + post-acute coordination)
Elder Law / Patient Advocate (civil action options if appropriate)
SEPSIS bundle:
Blood cultures × 2 + urine culture (DONE in ED; pending)
Cefepime 1 g IV q12h (renal dose-adjusted for Cr 2.4) + vancomycin per pharmacy/level (renal dose-adjusted)
Lactate trending q6h × 24 hr
MAP target ≥65; vasopressor (norepi) PRN if not achievable with fluid alone
Foley catheter for I/O + source control + accurate UOP monitoring (assess for removal post-resuscitation + once continent)
Sepsis re-assessment q4h with SOFA/qSOFA + clinical exam
ID consult for osteomyelitis (bone biopsy + culture planned post-debridement)
WOUND care:
Stage 4 sacral PI: cleansed with saline + packed with saline gauze + alginate for exudate + non-adherent secondary
Sharp debridement evaluation by wound surgery + possible OR debridement + possible flap reconstruction post-stabilization
Negative-pressure wound therapy (NPWT/wound vac) consideration post-debridement
Pressure-redistribution mattress + alternating pressure + low-air-loss as needed
Q2h repositioning AVOIDING sacrum + 30° side-lying angle + heel offloading
Smaller pressure injuries (Stage 2 heels + trochanter): appropriate dressings + offloading
Wound photography weekly per protocol
Pain assessment with appropriate analgesia (acetaminophen scheduled + opioid PRN for dressing changes)
NUTRITION (severe malnutrition + REFEEDING SYNDROME RISK):
REFEEDING SYNDROME PRECAUTIONS — patient has albumin 1.9 + prealbumin 6 + Phos 1.8 + K 3.1 + Mg 1.4 at admission
REPLACE phos + K + Mg BEFORE caloric load (target Phos ≥2.5, K ≥3.5, Mg ≥2.0)
THIAMINE 100 mg IV daily × 5 days BEFORE carbohydrate load (Wernicke prevention)
Start nutrition at 50% of calculated needs (20 kcal/kg = ~750 kcal/day initially)
Advance by 10% per day if electrolytes stable
Monitor phos + K + Mg + glucose daily during first week + with each advancement
Oral high-protein + caloric supplementation (Ensure 3×/day)
Vitamin C 500 mg daily + zinc 220 mg daily (wound healing)
Multivitamin daily
Tube feeding decision deferred — capacity-sensitive + medical indication + nutrition consult input
DEHYDRATION + AKI management:
Severe hypernatremia 152 — correct slowly at no more than 10 mEq/L per 24 hr to AVOID cerebral edema
IV fluid: hypotonic (D5 1/2 NS) calculated based on free water deficit + maintenance + ongoing losses
Hourly UOP target + I/O + daily weights
BMP q6h × 24 hr then q12h then daily
Trend Cr + UOP; renal dose-adjust antibiotics + medications
POLYPHARMACY review + medication reconciliation:
Restart with verified administration in hospital: levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (with TSH check), amlodipine 5 mg PO daily (hold if hypotension), atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily, donepezil 5 mg PO daily
Hold non-essential meds during sepsis stabilization
Deprescribing review at discharge
CAPACITY assessment:
Decision-specific capacity assessment by geriatric medicine + psychiatry as needed
SLUMS or MoCA when medically stable + sepsis resolved
Geriatric depression scale
Document capacity findings + decisions
Respect autonomy if capacity present; surrogate/conservatorship pathway if capacity absent
INTERVIEW + COMMUNICATION:
Spanish interpreter for all medical decisions + sensitive conversations (NOT family member as primary translator)
Private setting + caregiver not present for patient interviews
Open-ended + non-leading questions
Trauma-informed approach + neutral language
Verbatim quotes documented
Multiple interviews over time (patient may disclose more or less at different points)
DOCUMENTATION:
Objective + descriptive language (AVOID conclusory 'appears abused')
Body diagram with annotation of every finding (location + size + color + pattern + age)
Photography per institutional protocol with patient consent + scale + identifying marker + dated/timed + EMR secure
Verbatim patient + family + caregiver quotes in quotation marks
Source attribution ('patient stated...' 'son stated...' 'daughter stated...')
History-injury mismatch documented
Timeline of events documented
All consults + referrals + reports documented with date/time/recipient
Mandated reporting completion documented (SOC 341 form filed; APS intake number; law enforcement report number)
Confidentiality of reporter identity maintained (CA WIC §15633.5)
POST-ACUTE planning (capacity-sensitive + multidisciplinary):
Likely SNF for 4–8 weeks of wound healing + nutrition + functional rehabilitation
Then placement decision in coordination with patient + APS + social work + daughter
Options: SNF long-term, daughter's home with caregiver support + IHSS, restructured home environment with caregiver respite + adult day program + home health, alternative caregiver
Court protective order possible if active danger (filed by APS + court)
Caregiver stress for son: APS coordination + mental health/substance use referral if applicable + caregiver support
Patient autonomy respected if capacity present + chooses different path with safety planning + resources + monitoring
DAILY family meetings:
Structured + facilitated by social work + geriatric medicine
Include patient (with interpreter) + daughter (collaborative) + son (per APS investigation status + safety plan)
Explain medical findings + multidisciplinary team + APS investigation + safety plan + post-acute options
Honor patient voice + autonomy
Information sharing per HIPAA permission + need-to-know basis
TEAM debriefing + emotional support:
Caring for abuse/neglect victims takes emotional toll on staff
Post-event debriefing for clinical team
EAP + peer support + chaplain available
Recognize moral distress + provide space for processing + meaning-making
Sustainability of compassionate care requires team well-being
MONITORING:
Vital signs q4h (more frequent during sepsis resuscitation phase)
Cardiac monitoring (telemetry)
Hourly UOP × first 24 hr then q4h
Q2h repositioning + skin assessment with each turn
Daily weights
Q-shift skin assessment + Braden score
Daily labs + ABG as indicated
Wound photography weekly + with significant change
Pain assessment q4h + with each dressing change + as needed

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this ED admission?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically + ethically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: STAGE 4 SACRAL pressure injury 8 × 10 cm with visible bone + necrotic eschar + foul odor + purulent drainage — CHRONIC weeks-to-months evolution, NOT acute fall; multiple smaller pressure injuries (Stage 2 bilateral heels + R greater trochanter); contractures bilateral hands + R foot = months of immobility
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neglect cue. Stage 4 with visible bone cannot result from yesterday's fall (history-injury mismatch). Contractures take months to develop. Chronic neglect findings + likely osteomyelitis + requires wound + ID + nutrition + likely surgical intervention. Pressure injury Stage 3–4 in a cared-for patient is rare with appropriate prevention.

opts.text: BRUISING bilateral upper arms in 4-finger GRIP MARK PATTERN in DIFFERENT AGES (yellow-green 10–14 days + purple-blue 3–5 days = MULTIPLE EVENTS); bruising posterior thighs in oval/handprint pattern; bruising behind both ears (atypical for fall); healed scar R forehead not previously reported
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — physical abuse cue. Pattern + distribution + multiple ages of injuries inconsistent with single fall mechanism. Grip-mark bruising = restraint/handling. Behind-ears + posterior-thigh distribution atypical for accidental. Different ages = repeated events. RECOGNIZE + DOCUMENT objectively; AVOID conclusory language.

opts.text: Severe MALNUTRITION (27% weight loss from 52 → 38 kg over 18 mo + cachexia + temporal wasting + albumin 1.9) + severe DEHYDRATION (BUN/Cr 78/2.4 + hypernatremia 152 + oliguria) + untreated UROSEPSIS (cloudy/foul urine + lactate 4.8) + poor hygiene + soiled brief saturated estimated 12+ hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neglect cues + acute medical emergency. Severe malnutrition with refeeding syndrome risk (low phos/K/Mg at baseline — REPLACE BEFORE caloric load + thiamine BEFORE carbs). Sepsis bundle + cautious hypernatremia correction (max 10 mEq/L per 24 hr to avoid cerebral edema). Foley for source control + I/O. Multiple acute neglect-related complications requiring immediate medical management.

opts.text: PATIENT VERBATIM QUOTE (privately to ED RN with caregiver out of room + Spanish interpreter activated despite English-functional): 'My son gets angry. He doesn't mean to. But sometimes he grabs me. He's tired from caring for me.' Also: 'I don't want him in trouble. He's all I have.' Patient denied sexual abuse on direct supportive ask
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — patient disclosure. Verbatim quotes documented exactly + source-attributed. Privacy + separation from caregiver + professional interpreter (NOT family) per institutional protocol for sensitive interview even if patient English-functional. Patient's loyalty/fear of son does NOT change mandated reporting obligation — RN role is RECOGNIZE + DOCUMENT + REPORT; APS investigates.

opts.text: HISTORY-INJURY MISMATCH: son stated single fall yesterday; findings include chronic Stage 4 wound + months-old contractures + 27% weight loss + bruises of various ages + behind-ears + grip-mark bruising + untreated sepsis = cannot result from single acute event; daughter blocked from visiting × 6 months; last PCP visit 14 mo ago; medication non-administration evident on pill count
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pattern recognition. History-injury mismatch is the CENTRAL principle for recognizing abuse + neglect. Multiple chronic findings cannot be explained by single acute event. Social isolation (blocked daughter visits) + caregiver isolation + missed medical care + medication non-administration = pattern of neglect. RAISE reasonable suspicion threshold for mandated reporting.

opts.text: MANDATED REPORTING per CA WIC §15630: PHONE to APS within 24 hr (DONE 1830 ED Day 1 — intake number documented) + WRITTEN SOC 341 within 2 working days (DONE 0930 Day 2) + LAW ENFORCEMENT (suspected physical abuse with serious injury) — reasonable suspicion is threshold; patient consent NOT required (HIPAA exception); RN is mandated reporter in all 50 states; failure to report = misdemeanor with penalties up to 1 yr + $5,000 if death/great bodily injury results
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — mandated reporting is the most reliably tested NCLEX Ethical & Legal concept. REASONABLE SUSPICION is the threshold (NOT proof). PHONE within 24 hr + WRITTEN within 2 working days. NO patient consent required. MULTIPLE REPORTERS each independent. Mandated reporter immunity for good-faith reports. Failure-to-report penalties. RN role is RECOGNIZE + DOCUMENT + REPORT; APS investigates.

opts.text: SAFETY PLANNING: son visitation RESTRICTED pending APS investigation (handled by social work explanation to patient + family); security notified + bedside sign + shift report awareness; private room; photography per institutional protocol with consent; body diagram with annotation; verbatim quotes documented + source-attributed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — safety planning + documentation. Hospital is SAFE HAVEN; DO NOT discharge to suspected perpetrator without APS clearance + safety plan. Restricted visitation pending investigation. Photography + body diagram + verbatim quotes = objective documentation supporting BOTH patient safety + APS investigation. AVOID conclusory language.

opts.text: CAPACITY ASSESSMENT: patient oriented person + place + situation; baseline mild cognitive impairment per family + donepezil for MCI/early dementia; decision-specific capacity assessment in progress; capacity is decision-specific + cognitive impairment ≠ no capacity; mandated reporting is REQUIRED regardless of patient stated wishes; ongoing intervention/placement decisions respect autonomy if capacity present
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — autonomy-protection balance. Capacity is decision-specific + clinical (not legal competency). Cognitive impairment does NOT automatically eliminate capacity. Mandated reporting non-negotiable regardless of patient wishes. Ongoing placement + intervention navigate autonomy + protection. Patient's stated loyalty to son does NOT change reporting obligation but DOES inform ongoing planning if capacity present.

opts.text: INTERDISCIPLINARY TEAM activated: social work (lead coordinator) + geriatric medicine + wound care + nutrition (refeeding syndrome precautions) + ID (osteomyelitis) + PT/OT (contractures) + psychiatry + ethics + palliative care + chaplain + Spanish interpreter + pharmacy + case management + APS investigator + law enforcement officer + elder law/patient advocate; daughter as collaborative family resource; daily family meetings scheduled
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multidisciplinary coordination essential for complex elder abuse case. Each discipline contributes a piece. RN bedside role central in coordination + advocacy + documentation. Daughter as collaborative family member; son handled per safety plan + APS investigation. Daily family meetings facilitated by social work.

opts.text: Allergies: sulfa (rash); penicillin (rash per family report — never confirmed); document + avoid
opts.rat: Reassuring — allergy history documented. Document and proceed; avoid sulfa + verify PCN history when stable. Cefepime + vancomycin appropriate empiric coverage in PCN-allergic patient + sepsis. Not the priority cue at admission but important for safe medication management.

opts.text: Wait until APS investigation is complete + abuse is CONFIRMED before reporting + before restricting son's visitation; if reporting before confirmation, RN may face civil liability
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. REASONABLE SUSPICION (not proof or confirmation) is the threshold for mandated reporting per CA WIC §15630. RN is GRANTED IMMUNITY for good-faith reports per §15634. FAILURE to report when reasonable suspicion exists is a MISDEMEANOR with penalties. Safety planning + restricted visitation are immediate clinical safety decisions made independently of investigation outcome. Waiting for 'confirmation' = preventable delay that endangers patient + RN faces penalties for non-report.

opts.text: Daughter can serve as the primary Spanish interpreter for medical decisions + sensitive interviews because she is family, knows the patient, and it expedites communication while saving institutional resources
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. PROFESSIONAL MEDICAL INTERPRETER is required per institutional policy + Joint Commission standards + family preference + best-practice particularly for sensitive interviews. Family members as primary translators is INAPPROPRIATE due to: emotional bias, lack of medical terminology training, risk of mistranslation, ethical conflict + privacy concerns. THIS IS PARTICULARLY CRITICAL for abuse/neglect interviews where family dynamics are central to the case. Daughter participates as FAMILY not interpreter. (Even more critical: suspected perpetrator never used as interpreter.)

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What ethical + legal + clinical principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: MANDATED REPORTING per CA WIC §15630 + all 50 states: REASONABLE SUSPICION is threshold (NOT proof); patient consent NOT required (HIPAA 45 CFR 164.512(c) exception); PHONE within 24 hr + WRITTEN SOC 341 within 2 working days; APS for community-dwelling + ombudsman for facility + law enforcement for physical abuse; mandated reporter immunity for good-faith reports; failure-to-report misdemeanor with penalties up to 1 yr + $5,000 if death/GBI results
opts.rat: CORRECT — mandated reporting framework. Reasonable suspicion threshold (not proof). Consent not required (HIPAA exception). Timing + pathways + immunity + penalties. RN role: RECOGNIZE + DOCUMENT + REPORT; APS investigates. Multiple mandated reporters each independent (RN, MD, social worker, etc.).

opts.text: TYPES of elder abuse + characteristic signs: PHYSICAL (patterned bruising, defensive injuries, different-aged injuries, injuries inconsistent with history); NEGLECT (pressure injuries, dehydration, malnutrition, untreated conditions, poor hygiene); SEXUAL; EMOTIONAL/psychological; FINANCIAL; ABANDONMENT; ISOLATION; SELF-NEGLECT — multiple types can co-occur; recognition requires pattern not single finding
opts.rat: CORRECT — categories of elder abuse + recognition principles. Multiple types can co-occur (this case = physical + neglect + isolation likely). Pattern + history-injury mismatch + social context together raise reasonable suspicion. NO SINGLE FINDING is diagnostic; PATTERN matters.

opts.text: DIFFERENTIAL — distinguishing abuse from medical/accidental: SKIN FRAGILITY in elderly (purpura on dorsum of forearms/hands BILATERAL); ANTICOAGULANT/antiplatelet bruising; bleeding disorders; STEROID use; osteoporotic fractures (typical sites — vertebral, hip, distal radius); falls (typical mechanism + history-consistent); arthritis/contractures from inactivity; malnutrition from malignancy/depression/dementia; KEY DISTINGUISHERS = history-injury mismatch + pattern + distribution + multiple ages + delayed presentation + inconsistent history + social context + patient behavior
opts.rat: CORRECT — differential diagnosis framework. Medical mimics must be considered + differentiated. Pattern + history-injury mismatch + distribution + multiple ages of injuries + social context together distinguish. RN role: DESCRIBE OBJECTIVELY + document; AVOID conclusory adjudication — APS investigates.

opts.text: AUTONOMY-PROTECTION balance: CAPACITY is DECISION-SPECIFIC + clinical (not legal competency); cognitive impairment ≠ no capacity; if CAPACITY PRESENT + patient REFUSES intervention/placement away from suspected abuser — MANDATED REPORTING still REQUIRED; respect autonomy in ongoing planning + provide education + safety planning + monitoring; if CAPACITY ABSENT — surrogate decision-maker hierarchy or conservatorship
opts.rat: CORRECT — autonomy-protection balance framework. Capacity is decision-specific. Cognitive impairment does NOT automatically eliminate capacity. Mandated reporting is non-negotiable regardless of patient wishes (overrides autonomy for the reporting itself). Ongoing intervention navigates autonomy + protection. Patient loyalty/fear/dependence are common reasons capable victims refuse — DO NOT pressure or shame.

opts.text: TRAUMA-INFORMED interview + communication: SAFETY (private + caregiver not present); PROFESSIONAL INTERPRETER (NOT family); open-ended non-leading questions; direct but supportive ask about safety; neutral accepting language; verbatim quotes documented; respect pace; DO NOT INTERROGATE; avoid secondary victimization (patient is NOT to blame); information without coercion
opts.rat: CORRECT — trauma-informed care framework. Safety (separation from suspected perpetrator) first. Professional interpreter not family member. Open-ended non-leading questions. Direct but supportive. Avoid secondary victimization. RN role = OBSERVE + DOCUMENT + REPORT + SUPPORT — NOT investigate.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION principles: OBJECTIVE + DESCRIPTIVE (NOT conclusory); body diagrams with annotation; photography with consent per institutional protocol + scale + identifying marker + dated/timed + EMR secure; verbatim quotes in quotation marks; source attribution; history-injury mismatch + timeline; all consults + referrals + reports with date/time/recipient; mandated reporting completion documented; reporter identity confidential per CA WIC §15633.5
opts.rat: CORRECT — documentation framework. Objective + descriptive (not conclusory). Body diagram + photography + verbatim + source-attributed + timeline. Documentation supports both patient safety + APS investigation + potential legal proceedings. Confidentiality of reporter identity. AVOID 'appears abused' — DESCRIBE what observed.

opts.text: SAFETY PLANNING + post-acute placement: HOSPITAL as SAFE HAVEN; restricted visitation of suspected perpetrator pending APS; multidisciplinary case conference; post-acute options (SNF + alternative caregiver + adult day + IHSS + home health + assisted living + conservatorship); caregiver stress as CONTEXT (not excuse) — caregiver support + respite + mental health/substance use referral for recurrence prevention
opts.rat: CORRECT — safety planning + post-acute placement framework. Hospital safe haven + restricted visitation pending investigation + multidisciplinary coordination + multiple placement options. Caregiver stress addressed for recurrence prevention. DO NOT discharge to suspected perpetrator without APS clearance + safety plan.

opts.text: REFEEDING SYNDROME precautions in severe malnutrition: REPLACE phos + K + Mg BEFORE caloric load (target Phos ≥2.5, K ≥3.5, Mg ≥2.0); THIAMINE 100 mg IV daily × 5 days BEFORE carbohydrate load (Wernicke prevention); start nutrition at 50% of calculated needs (~20 kcal/kg = ~750 kcal/day initially); advance by 10% per day if electrolytes stable; monitor phos + K + Mg + glucose daily during first week; START LOW + GO SLOW; hypoPhosphatemia is the CARDINAL + most dangerous refeeding shift
opts.rat: CORRECT — refeeding syndrome framework. Severe malnutrition + low phos/K/Mg at baseline = high risk. REPLACE BEFORE feeding + thiamine BEFORE carbs + start low + go slow. HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA is the CARDINAL refeeding shift (the most life-threatening one — cardiac, respiratory, neurologic). This is the same principle as case-076 anorexia nervosa refeeding.

opts.text: Wait until abuse is CONFIRMED by APS investigation before reporting to avoid liability for false accusation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. REASONABLE SUSPICION (not confirmation or proof) is the threshold for mandated reporting. RN is GRANTED IMMUNITY for good-faith reports per CA WIC §15634. FAILURE to report when reasonable suspicion exists is a misdemeanor with penalties up to 1 yr + $5,000 if death/GBI results. Waiting for 'confirmation' = preventable delay + endangers patient + RN faces non-report penalties.

opts.text: Use family members (daughter or son) as the primary medical interpreter for sensitive abuse-related interviews because they know the patient and can expedite communication
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. PROFESSIONAL MEDICAL INTERPRETER is required + institutional + Joint Commission + best-practice particularly for sensitive interviews. Family members inappropriate due to emotional bias, lack of medical training, mistranslation risk, ethical conflict + privacy + role confusion. Suspected perpetrator NEVER used as interpreter. Family supportive members participate as FAMILY not as interpreter.

opts.text: Confront the son directly + demand explanation for the patient's injuries + tell him you will report him to APS unless he confesses + provides his version of events
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. RN role is RECOGNIZE + DOCUMENT + REPORT + SUPPORT — NOT interrogate or confront. Confrontation: (1) compromises APS investigation, (2) may escalate danger to patient, (3) interferes with rapport, (4) is not within RN scope of investigation, (5) violates confidentiality of mandated report. INSTEAD: document son's stated history neutrally + report to APS + APS investigates + law enforcement if active danger.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this admission
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Acute Sepsis + Hemodynamic Instability r/t untreated urosepsis from prolonged neglect; sepsis bundle (cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hr + 30 mL/kg fluid + MAP ≥65 + lactate + source control via Foley); cautious hypernatremia correction (max 10 mEq/L per 24 hr to avoid cerebral edema); REFEEDING SYNDROME precautions (replace phos/K/Mg BEFORE caloric load + thiamine BEFORE carbs)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Acute medical emergency — sepsis is the immediate life threat. Sepsis bundle + cautious hypernatremia correction + refeeding precautions are time-sensitive interventions. WITHOUT medical stabilization, all other concerns are moot. Foundation for all subsequent care + investigation.

opts.text: Safety + Mandated Reporting r/t suspected elder abuse + neglect; PHONE APS within 24 hr (DONE) + WRITTEN SOC 341 within 2 working days (DONE) + LAW ENFORCEMENT for suspected physical abuse with serious injury (DONE); RESTRICTED visitation of suspected perpetrator pending APS investigation; safety plan + security + private room; objective documentation + photography + body diagram + verbatim quotes
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Immediate safety + legal obligation. Reasonable suspicion threshold reached → MANDATED REPORTING within 24 hr is legally required + ethically non-negotiable. Hospital is safe haven during medical stabilization. Documentation supports both safety + investigation. Time-sensitive.

opts.text: Wound Care + Infection Management r/t Stage 4 sacral pressure injury with visible bone + likely osteomyelitis from prolonged neglect; wound debridement + offloading + dressings + nutrition + IV antibiotics (cefepime + vancomycin); bone biopsy + culture; expected 6+ weeks IV antibiotics; possible OR debridement + flap reconstruction
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Major source of morbidity + infection + ongoing medical management. Stage 4 with bone exposure + likely osteomyelitis requires aggressive multidisciplinary management. Nutrition + offloading + antibiotic + surgical evaluation. Foundation for healing + recovery.

opts.text: Capacity Assessment + Autonomy-Protection Balance + Family-Centered Communication r/t decision-specific capacity + baseline MCI + patient values (loyalty to son + reluctance to make him 'in trouble') + interdisciplinary engagement (social work + APS + ethics + palliative + chaplain) + daughter as collaborative family member + professional Spanish interpreter + daily family meetings
opts.rat: #4 — Significant + ongoing. Capacity-sensitive engagement + autonomy-protection balance + family-centered communication shape ongoing care + post-acute planning. Patient voice + values respected; mandated reporting non-negotiable but ongoing intervention navigates autonomy. Multidisciplinary engagement essential.

opts.text: Post-Acute Planning + Caregiver Stress Recognition r/t multidisciplinary case conference + SNF for 4–8 weeks wound healing + nutrition + functional rehabilitation + then placement decision (SNF long-term, daughter's home with caregiver support + IHSS, restructured home + adult day + home health, alternative caregiver); caregiver stress for son addressed via APS + mental health/substance use referral for recurrence prevention
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + at discharge. Post-acute planning ensures safe transition + prevents recurrence. Caregiver stress addressed for son via APS + supports. Multiple placement options. Patient autonomy respected if capacity present. DO NOT discharge to suspected perpetrator without APS clearance + safety plan. Foundation for safe long-term outcome.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient at this admission.

opts.text: Sepsis bundle within 1 hr: blood + urine cultures, cefepime + vancomycin IV broad empiric (renal dose-adjusted for Cr 2.4), 30 mL/kg crystalloid resuscitation, MAP ≥65 target, lactate trending q6h × 24 hr, source control via Foley + wound care, vasopressor (norepi) PRN if MAP not achievable with fluid alone
opts.rat: Acute sepsis from untreated urosepsis is the immediate medical emergency. Sepsis bundle is time-sensitive (within 1 hr antibiotics + fluid + MAP). Renal dose-adjusted antibiotics for AKI. Foundation for all subsequent care.

opts.text: MANDATED REPORTING per CA WIC §15630: PHONE to APS within 24 hr + WRITTEN SOC 341 within 2 working days + LAW ENFORCEMENT for suspected physical abuse with serious injury; reasonable suspicion threshold (NOT proof); patient consent NOT required (HIPAA 45 CFR 164.512(c) exception); reporter immunity per §15634
opts.rat: Mandated reporting is required when reasonable suspicion exists. Multiple reporters each independent. Time-sensitive (24 hr phone + 2 working days written). Reporter immunity for good-faith. Failure-to-report misdemeanor with penalties. CRITICAL ETHICAL + LEGAL obligation.

opts.text: REFEEDING SYNDROME precautions: REPLACE phos + K + Mg BEFORE caloric load (target Phos ≥2.5, K ≥3.5, Mg ≥2.0); THIAMINE 100 mg IV daily × 5 days BEFORE carbohydrate load (Wernicke prevention); start nutrition at 50% of calculated needs (~20 kcal/kg = ~750 kcal/day initially); advance by 10% per day if electrolytes stable; monitor phos + K + Mg + glucose daily during first week
opts.rat: Severe malnutrition + low baseline phos/K/Mg = HIGH refeeding syndrome risk. START LOW + GO SLOW. REPLACE BEFORE feeding + thiamine BEFORE carbs. Hypophosphatemia is the cardinal + most dangerous refeeding shift. Same principles as anorexia nervosa refeeding.

opts.text: SAFETY PLAN: son visitation RESTRICTED pending APS investigation (social work explanation to patient + family); security notified + bedside sign + shift report awareness; private room; photography per institutional protocol with consent + scale + identifying marker + dated/timed + EMR secure; body diagram with annotation; verbatim quotes documented + source-attributed; hospital as SAFE HAVEN
opts.rat: Immediate safety planning + objective documentation. Hospital safe haven. Restricted visitation pending APS investigation. Photography + body diagram + verbatim quotes support both safety + APS investigation. DO NOT discharge to suspected perpetrator without APS clearance + safety plan.

opts.text: WOUND care: Stage 4 sacral PI cleansed + packed; sharp debridement evaluation + possible OR debridement + flap reconstruction post-stabilization; NPWT consideration post-debridement; pressure-redistribution mattress + alternating pressure + low-air-loss; q2h repositioning AVOIDING sacrum + 30° side-lying + heel offloading; bone biopsy + culture for osteomyelitis; cefepime + vancomycin × 6+ weeks IV for osteomyelitis
opts.rat: Stage 4 with bone exposure + likely osteomyelitis requires aggressive multidisciplinary wound management. Debridement + offloading + dressings + nutrition + antibiotics + surgical evaluation. Foundation for healing.

opts.text: PROFESSIONAL Spanish medical interpreter for all medical decisions + sensitive interviews — daughter NOT used as primary translator per institutional + best-practice; suspected perpetrator NEVER used as interpreter; HIPAA + cultural humility + trauma-informed communication; private setting + caregiver not present for patient interviews
opts.rat: Professional interpreter is required per institutional + Joint Commission + best-practice + particularly critical for sensitive abuse-related interviews. Family members inappropriate as primary translators. Suspected perpetrator never used. Trauma-informed approach + private setting + neutral language.
